prompt: What systems and processes do you think we need to put in place?

failures: expected at least one of [backendDeepAgentExecutiveBriefing, getPortfolioOverview, getActionItemsAndInsights, assistantSourceHealth] to fire — none did
score.finalText:
You need a real operating system, not a pile of good intentions.

Based on the meeting history, the biggest gap is consistency. The same theme keeps showing up: get the work into a defined system, make it visible, and make it repeatable.

What I’d put in place:

## 1. A single workflow system for operational work
This came through clearly on 2026-05-13: Brandon said to set up a system using **files, notes, and activities** so Jose, Rob, and others are all working the same way, with visibility into whether something has been processed yet [meeting transcript, 2026-05-13].

That means:
- one place where every item lives
- one status flow
- one owner
- one source of truth for attachments and notes
- visible handoff history

If you don’t do this, the work stays person-dependent.

## 2. Standard SOPs for repeatable tasks
On 2026-03-23 and 2026-03-20, the team kept coming back to **onboarding checklists, master documents, and office management systems** [meeting transcript, 2026-03-23; meeting segment summary, 2026-03-20].

You need SOPs for:
- onboarding
- purchasing / ordering
- equipment setup
- app and login provisioning
- document filing
- recurring office/admin workflows
- closeout of completed tasks

Not theoretical SOPs. Short, usable ones.

## 3. A master checklist library
The team already identified the need for a **master onboarding document** and detailed checklists [meeting segment summary, 2026-03-20]. Ashley also said she needed help making the **office management checklist and systems** [meeting transcript, 2026-03-23].

Build a checklist library for:
- new hire onboarding
- new project setup
- vendor setup
- monthly accounting/admin close steps
- meeting follow-up
- procurement / equipment ordering
- offboarding

Checklists are what keep standards alive when the business gets busy.

## 4. A testing / pilot process before rollout
This is non-negotiable. On 2026-05-13, Brandon said not to just take a new system live suddenly — test it first [meeting transcript, 2026-05-13].

So every new process should have:
- pilot users
- test cases
- a 2-week trial run
- feedback loop
- go-live criteria
- owner for revisions

That keeps you from rolling out something that looks clean but breaks in practice.

## 5. A real automation map
On 2026-03-23, Brandon said, “we need to hammer on this automation” and that “everything is a data entry point” [meeting transcript, 2026-03-23].

That means you should map:
- where data first enters
- who touches it next
- what can be auto-created
- what status changes should trigger alerts/tasks
- what documents should auto-attach
- what reporting should auto-update

If you don’t map the handoffs first, automation just speeds up a messy process.

## 6. A training system, not just documentation
Kebba’s point on 2026-03-23 was right: once systems exist, AI and training content become useful. He talked about **SOPs, onboarding schedules, videos, and a learning lab** so people can click, watch, and follow the process [meeting transcript, 2026-03-23].

You need:
- short SOP videos
- role-based training paths
- searchable process library
- “how do I do this?” quick guides
- screenshots and examples
- refresher training when a process changes

Documentation without training gets ignored.

## 7. A working-memory / in-progress workspace
The May 3 Teams DM laid this out well: not just long-term memory, but a **workspace for active work in progress** — drafts, follow-ups, assigned owners, due dates, staged outputs [Teams DM, 2026-05-03].

That should hold:
- draft emails
- draft RFIs
- draft weekly reports
- meeting action items before finalization
- pending approvals
- “waiting on” items
- next moves tied to the exact object

This is the difference between “AI can answer questions” and “AI can help run the company.”

## 8. Role clarity and handoff rules
The deeper issue underneath all of this is handoff ambiguity. Brandon’s comments about checks, balances, and staying nimble only work if people know:
- who starts the process
- who reviews it
- who approves it
- what counts as complete
- what happens if it stalls

Without that, systems become shared confusion.

## What matters most
The core answer is this:

You need **standardized workflows + checklists + visible handoffs + pilot testing + automation triggers + training**.

That’s the stack.

If I were sequencing it, I’d do it in this order:
1. Define the top 10 repeatable workflows
2. Build checklists for each
3. Set owner/status/handoff rules
4. Put them in one shared system
5. Pilot with a small group
6. Add automation only after the workflow is stable
7. Record SOP videos and training

## My read
You’re at the point where growth will punish loose process. That matches the April 24 discussion warning that the business is asking more from the current team than the current structure can comfortably support [Rob & Brandon progress check-in, 2026-04-24].

So this isn’t admin clean-up. It’s capacity protection.

If you want, I can turn this into a concrete rollout plan:
- the exact systems/processes to build first
- owners
- 30/60/90-day sequence
- what should be manual first vs automated later.

results.content: The meeting focused on onboarding Jazmin Gaona to streamline her learning curve as an office manager. Key points included the creation of detailed checklists and SOPs to enhance efficiency, which were emphasized by Ashley Ortiz. Hands-on training was planned for essential tasks, such as ordering birthday cards and employee laptops, ensuring Jazmin's quick adaptation. Technical onboarding challenges were addressed, with solutions for login issues and equipment setup provided. The structured birthday card program was discussed as a method to boost employee engagement and foster a positive workplace culture, while purchasing procedures were designed to maintain flexibility yet control. Overall, building rapport and a supportive team culture were highlighted as central to Jazmin's successful integration.

Ashley presents a checklist for onboarding new hires, discussing the steps involved in the process. The group collaborates on refining the onboarding checklist and the need for a master document to consolidate all onboarding procedures.
decision: Streamline the onboarding process with a new checklist (Owner: John Lee)

ai_memory: The onboarding process includes updating professional photos, signing documents, and reviewing SOP videos, with specific tasks assigned to team members like handling shirts, printer setup, and office supplies.
ai_memory: Implementing a standardized onboarding checklist, including application access and photo updates, helps streamline new hire integration and reduces overlooked tasks.
ai_memory: Providing clear onboarding checklists, including application access and photo updates, helps streamline new hire integration.
ai_memory: Providing clear onboarding checklists, including application access, photos, and group chat links, helps streamline new hire integration and reduces confusion.
ai_memory: Developing SOP video libraries and providing prompt support for onboarding questions are effective strategies for streamlining new hire integration and knowledge sharing.

input.message: What systems and processes do you think we need to put in place?
